title: LAT ETS Flavor Model -- Listen to an Academic Talk

So_hook.example: So, you've probably noticed that many animals in nature are incredibly colorful, right?

Okay_alright.example: Okay, so today let's talk about a concept in ecology that really changed how we think...

Have_you_ever.example: Have you ever walked into a room and completely forgotten why you went there?

Let_me_introduce.example: Let me introduce you to a concept from anthropology that you probably experience every day...

opening_pattern.rule: Most lectures open with a conversational hook -- either 'So...' (36%), 'Okay/Alright...' (27%), or a rhetorical question (27%). Never start with a dry academic statement.

structure_pattern.description: Hook/intro -> Key concept defined -> Example/experiment -> Broader significance/implication

hook_intro.note: ALL lectures start with an engaging hook -- question, 'so', relatable scenario

concept_defined.note: Almost all lectures explicitly name and define the key concept

example_experiment.note: Most include a specific experiment, study, or real-world example

broader_significance.note: About half connect the concept to broader implications

next_topic_preview.note: Some end with a preview of the next class session

so.note: Most common -- used as opener and transition

now.note: Used to transition to new point

register.description: Conversational academic -- professor speaking to a class. NOT a written article read aloud. Uses contractions, addresses students directly, asks rhetorical questions.

examples: does that change how you perform?
what did I come here for?

subject_pool.psychology_heavy: Psychology topics (social, cognitive, neuroscience) = 55% of samples

detail.description: Specific facts stated in the lecture

inference.description: What can be inferred or implied

function.description: Why the professor mentions something specific

predict_next.description: What the class will discuss next (only when transcript previews it)

position_rules.Q1: ALWAYS main_idea (100% in samples). 'What is the lecture mainly about?'
position_rules.rule: Q1=main_idea, Q2-Q3=detail or function, Q4=inference or predict_next. Never put main_idea after Q1.

main_idea: What is the lecture mainly about?
detail: According to the professor, what is/are [X]?
What does the professor say about [X]?
What does the professor say about [topic]?
function: Why does the professor mention [specific detail]?
inference: What does the professor imply about [topic]?
What attitude does the professor express toward [topic]?
predict_next: What will the class most likely discuss in the next session?

option_design.WARNING: 67% of correct answers are the longest option in reference data -- this is a FLAW. Our generator MUST enforce <= 30% correct-is-longest rate.
option_design.WARNING_2: B is massively overrepresented (73%) in reference data. This is a practice material bias. Our generator MUST enforce even A/B/C/D distribution via pre-assignment.

for_detail_questions.description: Uses words from lecture but changes a key fact
for_detail_questions.example: A blue pigment found only in tropical species (wrong: no pigment at all)

for_detail_questions.description: Plausible for the academic field but not stated in lecture
for_detail_questions.example: Chemical reactions caused by UV light exposure (plausible but not what was said)
They break down more quickly than pigment-based colors (opposite of what was said)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Focuses on one detail instead of the overall topic
for_main_idea_questions.example: How factory lighting affects worker productivity (too specific)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Related academic topic but not THIS lecture
for_main_idea_questions.example: The chemical composition of mycorrhizal fungi (not the focus)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Uses keywords from the lecture in wrong context
for_main_idea_questions.example: Why car designers make vehicles look like they have faces (uses 'face' keyword misleadingly)

for_inference_questions.description: Takes a specific point too far
for_inference_questions.example: It was disproved entirely by recent research (too strong -- only partially revised)
It is always proportional to the species' population size (opposite of the lecture's point)

for_inference_questions.description: Valid inference but about wrong aspect
for_inference_questions.example: It is more common in children than in adults (no evidence for this in lecture)

for_function_questions.description: Misidentifies why the professor mentioned something
for_function_questions.example: To explain how elevator manufacturers use proxemics research (wrong reason)

for_function_questions.description: Assigns an argument the professor didn't make
for_function_questions.example: To argue that people should avoid using elevators (not the professor's intent)

for_function_questions.description: Restates what was said rather than explaining why
for_function_questions.example: To describe what happened in the experiment (restates, doesn't explain purpose)

tts_design.note: Academic talks are single-speaker (professor lecturing). Use lecture voice presets for slower pace, academic cadence.

generation_constraints.note: Lectures with 4 questions are very token-heavy. Keep batch size <= 3.
